text: This study highlights the utility of both exome and transcriptome sequencing data generated from pretreatment tumor samples for the identification of potential determinants of response to anti-PD-1. Although the overall somatic mutational loads of anti-PD-1-responsive melanoma tumors were not significantly higher than those of non-responsive tumors, higher mutational loads associated significantly with better survival after anti-PD-1 therapy. This finding is still consistent with the notion that neoepitopes derived from somatic non-synonymous mutations are critical for deriving clinical benefits from anti-PD-1 therapy in melanoma. However, objective tumor responses, although strongly associated with survival benefits, did not appear to be driven overwhelmingly by the overall somatic mutational loads. That is to say, a relatively low mutational load did not preclude a tumor response. This is consistent with findings from gastrointestinal cancers where low mutational loads did not preclude tumor infiltration by mutation-reactive, class I and II-restricted T cells ( Tran et al., 2015 ). Thus, overall somatic or predicted neoepitope loads of pretreatment melanoma tumors are not enough to predict response patterns to anti-PD-1 therapy.
summary: In melanoma, exome and transcriptome sequencing of pretreatment tumor samples is useful for identifying potential determinants of response to anti-PD-1. Neoepitopes derived from somatic non-synonymous mutations are indicated as critical for deriving clinical benefits from anti-PD-1 therapy. Although overall somatic mutational loads were not significantly higher in anti-PD-1–responsive tumors than in non-responsive tumors, higher mutational loads associated significantly with better survival after anti-PD-1 therapy. Objective tumor responses were strongly associated with survival benefits but were not overwhelmingly driven by overall somatic mutational loads. A relatively low mutational load did not preclude a tumor response, and overall somatic or predicted neoepitope loads of pretreatment melanoma tumors are not sufficient to predict response patterns to anti-PD-1 therapy.

text: PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade provides significant clinical benefits for melanoma patients. We analyzed the somatic mutanomes and transcriptomes of pretreatment melanoma biopsies to identify factors that may influence innate sensitivity or resistance to anti-PD-1 therapy. We find that, while overall high mutational loads associate with improved survival both in responding and non-responding patients, responding tumors are specifically enriched for mutations in the DNA repair gene BRCA2 . Innately resistant tumors display a transcriptional signature (referred to as the IPRES or I nnate anti- P D-1 Res istance) indicating concurrent upexpression of genes involved in the regulation of mesenchymal transition, cell adhesion, ECM remodeling, angiogenesis and wound-healing. Notably, MAPK-targeted therapy (MAPKi) induces similar signatures in melanoma, suggesting that a non-genomic form of MAPKi resistance mediates cross-resistance to anti-PD-1 therapy. Validation of the IPRES in other independent tumor cohorts defines a transcriptomic subset across distinct types of advanced cancer. These findings suggest that attenuating the biological processes that underlie IPRES may improve anti-PD1 response in melanoma and other cancer types.
summary: PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade provides significant clinical benefits for melanoma patients. In pretreatment melanoma biopsies, responding tumors were enriched for BRCA2 mutations and high mutational load associated with improved survival. Innately resistant tumors display the IPRES transcriptional signature that denotes up‑expression of genes regulating mesenchymal transition, cell adhesion, ECM remodeling, angiogenesis and wound healing. MAPK‑targeted therapy induces similar signatures in melanoma, suggesting non‑genomic MAPKi resistance mediates cross‑resistance to anti‑PD‑1 therapy. Validation across independent tumor cohorts defines IPRES across advanced cancers and suggests attenuating IPRES‑related processes may improve anti‑PD‑1 response.

text: PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade therapy induces a high rate of anti-melanoma response and provides unprecedented clinical benefits ( Hamid et al., 2013 ; Topalian et al., 2012 ). This therapeutic approach has also been shown to be active against a growing list of human malignancies, and clinical testing of combinations of PD-1 (or PD-L1) with other treatment targets has already begun ( Sharma and Allison, 2015 ). However, effective clinical use of anti-PD-1 agents is encumbered by a high rate of innate resistance (60-70%) in advanced metastatic melanoma. The mechanistic basis for the variation in response patterns or long-term clinical benefits (i.e., survival) remains poorly explained.
summary: PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade therapy induces a high rate of anti-melanoma response and provides unprecedented clinical benefits in melanoma patients. However, in advanced metastatic melanoma there is a high rate of innate resistance (60–70%) and the mechanistic basis for variation in response patterns or long-term clinical benefits remains poorly explained.
